metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm which ACs (if any) remain unimplemented: `check_import_cycles.py`, `.import-cycle-baseline.json`, the CI gate, and the `reducer`→`_ids` inversion fix all appear already shipped — update the plan to mark completed items and narrow the scope to only the genuinely remaining work (likely just AC #3: routing remaining leaf-module facade back-edges through `rebar._reads`).
findings.finding: AC #1 (import-linter CI contract) and AC #2 (reducer inversion fix) describe work that already exists in the codebase: `scripts/check_import_cycles.py` + `.import-cycle-baseline.json` + CI wiring in `.github/workflows/test.yml` + `tests/unit/test_import_cycles.py` are fully implemented, and `src/rebar/_ids.py` + the `resolver.py` shim + `reducer/_processors.py` importing from `rebar._ids` are already committed. Presenting shipped code as future work risks NIH-style re-implementation by a future author who misreads the plan scope.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Current state' section to the plan noting: AC #1 is DONE (`scripts/check_import_cycles.py` + `.github/workflows/test.yml` + `tests/unit/test_import_cycles.py`); AC #2 is DONE (`rebar._ids` leaf + `resolver.py` shim + `reducer/_processors.py` fix + `test_no_reducer_engine_support_cycle.py`). Retain AC #3 as the open item, and tighten its scope by listing which specific leaf modules still `import rebar` (the facade) for reads — the baseline shows `_reads::rebar`, `_commands::_reads`, `_io::_reads` pairs still present as cycle contributors.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether AC2 ('reducer no longer imports _engine_support; shared resolution in a leaf module') is already satisfied by the existing `rebar._ids` + `_engine_support/resolver.py` re-export shim, and either close this work item or describe what incremental work remains.
findings.finding: The plan presents the reducer layering inversion fix (AC2: 'Extract the shared id/alias resolution primitive into a LEAF module') as future work, but the codebase shows it is ALREADY DONE: `src/rebar/_ids.py` exists as the stdlib-only leaf, `_engine_support/resolver.py` is already a re-export shim over `_ids`, `reducer/_processors.py` imports from `rebar._ids` (not `_engine_support`), and `check_import_cycles.py` already has `reducer→_engine_support` listed as a FORBIDDEN_LAYER_IMPORT.
findings.location: What section item 2 + AC2: 'Fix the reducer layering inversion'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that AC1 is already satisfied by `scripts/check_import_cycles.py` + `.import-cycle-baseline.json` + CI wiring in `.github/workflows/test.yml`, and update the plan to describe only the delta work (if any) rather than presenting this as new work.
findings.finding: The plan describes the 'Import-linter (or grimp-based) contract in CI' (AC1) as something to be created, but `scripts/check_import_cycles.py` already exists, is already wired into CI (`.github/workflows/test.yml` line 146 runs `python scripts/check_import_cycles.py`), already has a committed baseline (`.import-cycle-baseline.json`), and is already tested with both synthetic and real-tree tests in `tests/unit/test_import_cycles.py` covering exactly the AC1 scenarios.
findings.location: What section item 1 + AC1: 'Import-linter CI contract'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether Item 2 (reducer inversion fix) is already complete in the codebase and, if so, update the plan to describe it as a completed prerequisite rather than a planned change — or identify what remaining work (e.g., ratcheting the baseline with `--update`) still needs doing.
findings.finding: The plan describes the reducer layering inversion fix ('Extract the shared id/alias resolution primitive into a LEAF module both depend on') as planned future work, but `reducer/_processors.py:244` already reads `from rebar._ids import resolve_ticket_id` and `_engine_support/resolver.py` is already a re-export shim — the fix is already committed. The plan's description of the current state is factually stale, making Item 2 a hallucinated edit target (there is nothing left to change).
findings.location: Plan section 'What', Item 2: 'Fix the reducer layering inversion'
findings.suggested_fix: Move Item 2 to a 'Already Completed' section and add a note that AC2 should be verified by running `python scripts/check_import_cycles.py` (the FORBIDDEN_LAYER_IMPORTS rule already encodes it). If any residual work remains (e.g., a baseline ratchet to shrink `largest_scc_size`), state it explicitly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that Item 1 (import-linter CI contract) is already implemented via `scripts/check_import_cycles.py` + `tests/unit/test_import_cycles.py` and update the plan to reflect pre-existing implementation, clarifying what (if anything) remains — e.g., running `--update` to ratchet the baseline after the other fixes land.
findings.finding: The plan describes the import-linter CI contract (Item 1) as future work ('Use import-linter/grimp or a custom grimp-based baseline check'), but `scripts/check_import_cycles.py`, `.import-cycle-baseline.json`, `tests/unit/test_import_cycles.py`, and the CI wiring in `.github/workflows/test.yml:146` all already exist and are fully implemented. This makes Item 1 a hallucinated edit target with no remaining work.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the SCC size claim against the live `.import-cycle-baseline.json` (currently shows `largest_scc_size=36`, `total_modules_in_cycles=49`) and update the Context section to reflect current, not historical, state.
findings.finding: The plan asserts a '~69-module strongly-connected import component' but the committed baseline (.import-cycle-baseline.json) shows largest_scc_size=36 and total_modules_in_cycles=49 — meaning the premise is off by roughly 2x and may describe a stale pre-fix state.
findings.location: Context section: 'A verified AST+Tarjan analysis found a ~69-module strongly-connected import component'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '~69-module strongly-connected import component' with the current committed baseline values (largest SCC: 36 modules, 49 total modules in cycles, 5 cyclic SCCs).

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Cross-check the named SCC members ('graph', 'signing', etc.) against the current `.import-cycle-baseline.json` cross_package_cycle_pairs and correct the Context section to name only packages actually in cross-package cycles.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the SCC spans 'graph' as one of the named subsystems ('spanning the facade, `_cli`, `_commands`, `graph`, `signing`, and most of `llm`'), but the committed `.import-cycle-baseline.json` cross_package_cycle_pairs contains no `graph::` entries, indicating `graph` is NOT currently part of any cross-package import cycle.
findings.location: Context section: 'spanning the facade, `_cli`, `_commands`, `graph`, `signing`, and most of `llm`'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the SCC member list with the actual baseline pairs: `_commands`, `_io`, `_reads`, `llm`, `rebar` (facade), `_engine_support`, `signing`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the specific files targeted by Item 3 (facade-read reroute) and confirm each is a read-only caller; explicitly exclude mixed read/write callers such as `llm/workflow/steps.py`, `llm/pai_tools.py` from scope.
findings.finding: AC3 ('Leaf modules that imported the rebar facade only for reads use rebar._reads') does not enumerate which specific files are in scope. Many `import rebar` callers in `llm/` use BOTH reads (`rebar.show_ticket`, `rebar.list_tickets`) AND writes or signing calls (`rebar.comment` in `pai_tools.py:172`, `workflow/steps.py:282`; `rebar.tag`/`rebar.edit_ticket` in `workflow/steps.py:300,319`). These mixed callers cannot be rerouted to `rebar._reads` and are not 'read-only leaf modules'. The plan does not distinguish them from pure-read callers.
findings.location: AC3: 'Leaf modules that imported the rebar facade only for reads use rebar._reads'
findings.suggested_fix: Candidates for pure-read reroute: `llm/eval_solver.py` (top-level `import rebar` at line 21, uses `rebar.show_ticket`/`rebar.list_tickets` only after inspection), `llm/spec_scan.py` (lazy `import rebar` for show/list only). Confirm by grepping each file for non-read `rebar.*` calls before including it in scope.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a measurable structural criterion to AC3: specify which cross-package cycle pairs (e.g. `llm::rebar`) the reroute is expected to remove from `.import-cycle-baseline.json`, and require a `--update` baseline commit that documents the improvement; without this the AC is verifiable only behaviorally, not structurally.
findings.finding: AC3's mechanism implies routing `llm.*` lazy `import rebar` calls through `rebar._reads` will remove facade back-edges from the import cycle, but `rebar._reads` itself imports from `rebar` (the facade) for `config` and `RebarError`, so the `_reads::rebar` cycle pair in the committed baseline persists after the reroute — the llm→_reads edge replaces llm→rebar but _reads→rebar keeps _reads in the same SCC. The plan does not acknowledge this partial effectiveness, and AC3 has no measurable cycle-reduction criterion (no 'baseline shrinks by N pairs' or 'llm::rebar pair removed'), making it impossible to verify the remediation succeeded beyond 'suite green'.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'Leaf modules that imported the `rebar` facade only for reads use `rebar._reads`; no behavior change; suite green.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate (or describe a discoverable set of) the leaf modules to be rerouted from `import rebar` to `rebar._reads`, or add an AC that verifies zero remaining facade-only-read imports via a grep/import-linter rule.
findings.finding: AC3 ('leaf modules that imported the rebar facade only for reads use rebar._reads') does not enumerate which modules are affected, leaving callers that depend on the old import path unacknowledged.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'A grep or import-linter check confirms no leaf module in the identified set still imports the top-level rebar facade solely for read operations.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that asserts the SCC is measurably smaller after all three changes (e.g., 'grimp/import-linter reports fewer than N cross-package edges in the SCC, or the SCC is split into smaller components'), so 'reduce' is end-state-observable and not just implied.
findings.finding: The title promises to 'Reduce the import-cycle SCC' but no AC verifies the SCC is measurably smaller after all three changes. AC1 only proves forward-prevention (new cycles blocked), not backward-reduction. AC2 and AC3 fix two specific inversions but the combined reduction in SCC membership is never measured or asserted.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — all three ACs combined
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC: 'The import graph (measured via grimp) shows the SCC has fewer than 69 members after the inversion fix and facade-read reroute; record before/after module counts in the PR description.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan (or expand-contract sequencing) for each of the three structural changes: (1) import-linter baseline can be temporarily widened via a named escape hatch, (2) the leaf-module extraction is committed as a separate, individually-revertable commit before the reducer import removal, (3) the facade reroute is a single atomic commit with a documented revert command.
findings.finding: The plan restructures import paths for three components (import-linter contract, reducer/_processors.py extraction, facade-to-_reads reroute) but provides no explicit rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract sequencing for any of them. 'Suite green' is a validation gate, not a reversibility mechanism.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all four ACs) and What items 1–3
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback' subsection: (1) The import-linter baseline file is version-controlled; widening it temporarily is the escape hatch. (2) Land the leaf-module creation commit first (purely additive), then the reducer import-removal commit — each is independently revertable. (3) The facade reroute commit touches only import lines and is revertable with `git revert <sha>` with no data migration needed — document this explicitly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly sequence delivery: ship the import-linter CI contract (Item 1) as the first commit/PR, then apply the reducer inversion fix (Item 2) and rebar._reads reroute (Item 3) as subsequent commits protected by the gate.
findings.finding: The plan sequences all three remediations as a single flat batch with no explicit ordering, missing the opportunity to de-risk via a thin vertical-slice MVP: the CI gate (Item 1) should ship before the refactors (Items 2 and 3) so that the gate itself validates each subsequent change. As written, the refactors could merge before the gate exists, providing no regression protection, and the gate could ship after the refactors with no proof it would have caught anything.
findings.location: What — items 1, 2, 3; Acceptance Criteria — all four bullets treated as co-equal

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that 'import-linter' (the pip package) cannot implement the grandfathered/ratchet baseline pattern natively — specify that the delivery vehicle is a custom grimp-based script (or confirm the existing scripts/check_import_cycles.py already satisfies this AC), not the import-linter contract DSL.
findings.finding: The plan presents 'import-linter (the pip package) or grimp-based baseline check' as equivalent alternatives, but the import-linter pip package does not support a grandfathered/ratcheted cycle baseline — it enforces only absolute contracts. This is an unverified capability assertion: if a future implementer picks 'import-linter' (the pip package) as the delivery vehicle, they will find it cannot implement the strangler pattern the plan describes, and will have to fall back to a custom grimp script anyway.
findings.location: What section, Item 1: 'Use import-linter/grimp or a custom grimp-based baseline check'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'Use import-linter/grimp or a custom grimp-based baseline check' with 'Use a custom grimp-based baseline check (grimp>=3 is already a [dev] dep; the import-linter pip package does not support a grandfathered ratchet natively).' If the existing scripts/check_import_cycles.py already fully satisfies AC1, note that explicitly so the AC can be marked done.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for AC1's 'fails on new cycle' property (e.g., `import-linter --config .importlinter` against a temporary synthetic bad import), and name the config file path and CI step where the contract lives.
findings.finding: AC1's proof that the CI gate catches new cycles is not a concrete, runnable command — 'proven by a test/among the contract's own checks' is a hedge, not a reproducible invocation. An executor cannot verify the gate works without knowing what command to run and what artifact demonstrates failure on a synthetic bad import.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1: 'passes on current state, fails a newly-introduced cross-package cycle (proven by a test/among the contract's own checks)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a step: 'Create `.importlinter` (or `setup.cfg` `[importlinter]` section) at repo root; add a single-contract entry grandfathering the current baseline; prove the gate fires by temporarily adding `import rebar` to a leaf module, running `lint-imports`, confirming non-zero exit, then reverting — include this canary run as part of the CI contract PR.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add or update an ADR / CLAUDE.md section documenting the layering policy (why `reducer` must not import `_engine_support`, what `rebar._reads` is for, and how to maintain the import-linter baseline) so future contributors can reason about and uphold the contract.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel layering contract (import-linter CI gate), a new shared leaf module (id/alias resolution primitive), and a new `rebar._reads` facade-bypass pattern — but does not include an update to any architectural decision record (ADR), CLAUDE.md, or design doc to capture WHY this layering policy exists and how future contributors should maintain it.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief ADR or a `## Layering Policy` section to CLAUDE.md covering: (1) the import-linter gate purpose and how to update the baseline intentionally, (2) the leaf-module contract for id/alias resolution, and (3) the `rebar._reads` vs `rebar` facade distinction.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit negative-test step to the CI contract AC: commit a canary file that introduces a deliberate cross-package import violation, run the linter, assert non-zero exit (failure), then revert — this must be a reproducible CI step or a dedicated test, not just 'the contract's own checks'.
findings.finding: The import-linter contract AC ('proven by a test/among the contract's own checks') is ambiguous and may resolve to a self-authored-oracle/snapshot pattern: a grandfathered baseline that records current imports will pass trivially on current state, but without an explicit negative test (deliberately introduce a violating cross-package import, run the linter, assert failure, revert), the contract never proves it would catch a new violation. The phrase 'among the contract's own checks' could mean the gate IS the snapshot, which is a tautological check that locks in the current (broken) state and passes even if the tooling is misconfigured.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — first bullet: 'Import-linter (or grimp-based) contract in CI: passes on current state, fails a newly-introduced cross-package cycle (proven by a test/among the contract's own checks).'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a step in the CI job (or a pytest/shell test) that: (1) temporarily appends a known-bad import to a leaf module, (2) runs `lint-imports` (or the grimp check), (3) asserts exit code ≠ 0, (4) reverts the change. This makes the 'fails a newly-introduced cycle' criterion concrete and prevents tautological pass.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/eval_solver.py` (top-level `import rebar` at line 21) to the explicit list of Item 3 reroute targets, and verify it uses only read-side facade APIs before committing the change.
findings.finding: `src/rebar/llm/eval_solver.py` has a top-level (non-lazy) `import rebar` at line 21 — a module-level facade import that contributes to the SCC at static-analysis time. This is a concrete out-of-`llm`-dir consumer of the facade that the plan does not enumerate, and it is the strongest single candidate for the `_reads` reroute since its usage appears to be read-only (`rebar.show_ticket`/`rebar.list_tickets`). Its omission from the plan means the blast-radius of Item 3 is underspecified.
findings.location: Plan section 'What', Item 3: 'Route leaf reads through rebar._reads'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `import rebar` at `eval_solver.py:21` with `from rebar._reads import show_ticket, list_tickets` (and update call sites accordingly), or make it a lazy import inside the functions that need it — whichever better preserves the `import rebar.llm` optionality contract documented in the file.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add one sentence of positive rationale to AC3/What-3 explaining that `rebar._reads` (not `rebar._engine_support.reads` directly) is the correct target because it enforces the uniform read-freshness policy (`ensure_fresh` throttle) across all call sites.
findings.finding: The plan provides no positive rationale for why `rebar._reads` is the right routing target for AC3 rather than a direct import of `rebar._engine_support.reads` (the actual implementation layer). A reader implementing AC3 has no stated reason to prefer the `_reads` shim and might reasonably bypass it, losing the read-freshness (ensure_fresh) policy that `_reads` enforces uniformly.
findings.location: ## What — item 3: 'Route leaf reads through `rebar._reads` instead of `import rebar` (the facade), removing a class of facade back-edges.'
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the AC3 description: 'Use `rebar._reads` (not `rebar._engine_support.reads` directly) because `_reads` enforces the uniform read-freshness policy (`ensure_fresh`) that the bare reads layer does not apply at call sites — preserving the throttled git-fetch behavior across CLI, library, and MCP surfaces.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
